does: This paper introduces MIRAGE, a benchmark of 1,200 prompts designed to audit anti-Muslim bias in frontier large language models under realistic deployment conditions. Rather than evaluating models only on single-turn completion tasks, the work tests six state-of-the-art models across three conditions: direct completion, chain-of-thought reasoning, and simulated agentic decision-making in high-stakes scenarios including content moderation, lending decisions, refugee claim processing, and hiring. The key findings reveal that chain-of-thought reasoning paradoxically amplifies Muslim-violence associations by 12–34% compared to direct completion, agentic systems show 9–22 percentage-point bias gaps between Muslim and non-Muslim cases on identical evidence, and bias intensifies 18–27% when models retrieve recent conflict-related news. The work also demonstrates that existing prompt-based bias mitigation techniques fail to transfer effectively across these three deployment contexts.
connects: This work extends five years of prior research documenting anti-Muslim bias in language models, building on foundational studies that first identified persistent associations between Muslim identity and violence in LLM outputs. The paper responds to a critical gap in evaluation methodology: while earlier work assessed bias primarily through isolated single-turn completions, it recognizes that deployed LLMs now function as reasoning systems and decision-making agents in real-world applications. The benchmark design incorporates insights from recent work on chain-of-thought reasoning and emergent capabilities, while also drawing on research examining how retrieved external context shapes model behavior. The evaluation methodology connects to broader work on fairness auditing and bias measurement in AI systems, particularly studies investigating how architectural choices and inference procedures amplify or mitigate demographic harms.
enables: This work establishes a foundation for deeper investigation into how reasoning and agentic capabilities interact with demographic biases in frontier models. It opens questions about why chain-of-thought reasoning amplifies rather than mitigates bias, pointing toward research on mechanistic explanations for bias emergence in reasoning chains. The time-coupling finding suggests future work examining how retrieval-augmented systems can be designed to reduce news-driven stereotyping while maintaining factual grounding. The multi-condition evaluation framework provides a template for auditing other demographic groups across realistic deployment scenarios, enabling more comprehensive fairness assessments. The poor transfer of existing mitigations indicates a need for context-aware debiasing methods that maintain effectiveness across direct completion, reasoning, and agentic conditions, establishing a research direction for developing robust bias mitigation at scale in frontier systems.
